lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market independent offices that repeatedly provide external customers with the NAICS-defined services of drafting, approving, executing, receiving, indexing, or storing legal documents.
lens.excluded_activities: Law firms, title abstract and settlement offices, internal corporate units, financing vehicles, and signature-only notary public services that NAICS places in 541199.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External customers pay per document matter or under a repeat service relationship; this screen excludes one-off consumer stamp-only work unless the firm also supplies the NAICS-defined document service on a repeat outsourced basis.

a.bridge: S2 reports that a legal-profession study estimated 44 percent of tasks susceptible to automation, rather than measuring NAICS 541120. I translate that legal-services proxy downward for the required human identity, execution, and custody work in the NAICS definition; the proxy likely overstates substitution for this industry.
a.rationale: Document intake, classification, drafting support, retrieval, and routine correspondence are exposed, but execution and accountability require human review. The base is below the cited legal-services task proxy.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task-and-occupation survey of 541120 offices, including time spent on document drafting, custody, customer intake, and formal execution, would materially change this estimate.
caveats: No current occupation or task mix is published for this suppressed and unusually narrow NAICS industry.
The legal-profession proxy includes lawyers and paralegals with work unlike this lens.

rho.rationale: Remote delivery is legally available in most states, enabling workflow change, but legal accuracy, identity proofing, audiovisual records, credential analysis, and security rules leave a meaningful human-controlled implementation boundary.
rho.change_evidence: A representative survey of 541120 operators on remote-notarization adoption, AI workflow use, error rates, and time savings, segmented by state, would materially change this estimate.
caveats: The state-law evidence establishes permitted technology and controls, not actual AI adoption or savings in this NAICS.
Requirements vary by state and document type.

e.rationale: No defensible count identifies which 541120 firms are both in the normalized EBITDA band and provide a recurring or repeat outsourced service under the frozen lens.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level revenue, EBITDA, customer-concentration, and repeat-revenue data from a business-information vendor or operator interviews would permit an eligibility estimate.
caveats: The frozen n input is unavailable by instruction and must remain dataset-injected MISSING.
NAICS classification alone does not establish recurring revenue or normalized EBITDA.

s5.rationale: No defensible source measures five-year qualifying control transfers for eligible 541120 firms, and the public evidence found does not identify owner succession for this population.
s5.change_evidence: A longitudinal business-owner dataset matched to 541120 firms, or transaction and succession records from specialist M&A intermediaries, would permit an estimate.
caveats: General small-business exit statistics would not distinguish transfers from closures or internal reorganizations.

q.rationale: Per-matter billing can initially retain some productivity benefit, but standardized technology, low switching friction, and regulated notarial pricing constrain durable retention. Florida caps the online notarial-act fee at $25 while allowing separate provider charges, illustrating both pricing constraint and partial pass-through risk.
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts, realized price changes after workflow automation, and state-by-state fee schedules from a representative set of operators would materially change this estimate.
caveats: Florida's fee rule is not a national fee survey and does not govern non-notarial document work.
Actual fee structure and competition vary by state and customer type.

d5.bridge: S2 projects legal-services employment growth of 1.6 percent from 2023 to 2033, not constant-price demand for NAICS 541120. I use this slow legal-services trend as a five-year directional proxy for the current document-service basket; it may overstate demand if remote platforms displace operators, or understate it if document activity grows faster than legal employment.
d5.rationale: The available legal-services proxy indicates near-flat long-run activity. The range allows for document-transaction cyclicality and substitution by remote platforms while retaining the need for legal-document processing.
d5.change_evidence: A constant-dollar revenue and transaction series specifically for 541120, split among real-estate, estate, contract, and record-custody matters, would materially change this estimate.
caveats: Legal-services employment is a broader population and is not a volume measure.
A direct five-year forecast for this NAICS was not found.

o.rationale: A large share should still require an accountable commissioned operator because remote notarization retains personal appearance, identity verification, credential analysis, proofing, recording, and security controls. Software can remove coordination and document handling from some current work, so this is below full retention.
o.change_evidence: State-level records showing remote-notarization volumes, platform market share, operator staffing per transaction, and acceptance by end users would materially change this estimate.
caveats: The evidence is about remote notarization generally, while this NAICS excludes signature-only notary services and includes broader document work.
Future changes in state law or identity technology could alter the operator requirement.

narrative.executive_view: Offices of Notaries is an unusually narrow and poorly measured NAICS category. Its defined work combines legal-document processing with accountable execution and custody, but the ordinary signature-only notary market belongs in another industry. The missing firm-eligibility and transfer evidence is a central limitation.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can support intake, document classification, drafting assistance, retrieval, and customer communication. A legal-services task proxy suggests material exposure, yet the formal act still depends on accurate review and a person responsible for identity and execution.
narrative.value_capture: Per-matter pricing offers some initial retention of workflow savings, but standardized remote platforms and regulated fee rules create pass-through pressure. A Florida online notarial act is capped at $25, while related platform services may be billed separately.
narrative.firm_availability: No reliable public evidence identifies firms in the frozen EBITDA band that also have repeat outsourced revenue, nor does it measure qualifying control transfers. This is not treated as a zero opportunity; it remains missing evidence.
narrative.demand_durability: The broad legal-services outlook is slow but positive, supporting a near-flat demand proxy. Remote authorization in 47 states and the District of Columbia changes delivery rather than eliminating the legal-document need, although platform substitution can reduce operator volume.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The NAICS definition and the common meaning of notary do not align cleanly: signature-only notary services are classified in 541199. State-specific rules, error liability, identity requirements, customer acceptance, and sparse industry data make extrapolation particularly uncertain.
narrative.principal_driver: Document-processing workflows have real automation exposure while the formal act remains accountable and regulated.
narrative.principal_weakness: There is no defensible public evidence for eligible LMM-firm share or five-year qualifying transfer probability in this narrow NAICS.

sources.title: Census Bureau NAICS Sector 54 definition
sources.supports: Defines 541120 as offices drafting, approving, executing, receiving, indexing, and storing legal documents; states that signature-only notary public services are classified in 541199.

sources.title: BLS: Incorporating AI impacts in employment projections
sources.supports: Reports a study estimating 44 percent of legal-profession tasks susceptible to automation and projects legal-services employment growth of 1.6 percent from 2023 to 2033; also describes continuing legal-review needs.

sources.title: National Association of Secretaries of State: Remote Electronic Notarization
sources.supports: States that 47 states and the District of Columbia allow remote e-notarization and describes personal appearance, identity verification, audiovisual communication, credential analysis, and privacy standards.

sources.title: Florida Statutes Section 117.265, Online notarization procedures
sources.supports: Requires online identity confirmation, audiovisual communication, remote government identification presentation, credential analysis, identity proofing, and secure communication for Florida online notarizations.

sources.title: Florida Statutes Section 117.275, Fees for online notarization
sources.supports: Caps the fee for an online notarial act at $25 and permits separate charges for services other than the act, including a RON service provider.
